text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic splenectomy, using minimally invasive techniques to remove the spleen.
The surgeon begins by freeing the ligaments surrounding a large recurrent cyst in the spleen. As the peritoneal attachments are released, the cyst is visibly emptied.
The surgeon continues to empty the contents of the large cyst in the spleen, using laparoscopic instruments to facilitate the process.
The surgeon uses the ERBI bipolar radio frequency guided instrument to take the splenic artery, effectively freeing the spleen from its attachments to the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon dissects and deliberately cuts the splenic artery, which is visibly separated from surrounding structures. The ERBI bipolar radio frequency guided instrument is used to free the spleen and the large cyst encircling it.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to dissect and free the diaphragmatic attachments of the spleen, completing its detachment.
The surgeon performs the final step of the laparoscopic splenectomy, which involves freeing the spleen from its attachments, a step that is typically the first in open surgery.

The surgeon carefully dissects the remaining attachments of the spleen from the diaphragm, ensuring the large spleen and cyst do not obstruct the camera view. The spleen is then removed through a small incision.
During the laparoscopic splenectomy, the surgeon observes the pulsating splenic artery while preparing to remove the perisplenic giant cyst.
